raw_step.observation: Total open beds were 67, which makes a full 70-patient in-hospital assignment infeasible. Enumeration found the unique feasible full-bed allocation A=22, B=39, C=6, leaving 3 overflow patients. The lower-flag pattern A=0, B=17, C=6 was also identified as a sensitivity case but it only accepts 23 patients.
raw_step.operation: derive bed headroom, nurse proxies, flag thresholds, and integer allocation optimum
raw_step.reasoning: The primary objective is to minimize risk while staying within physical capacity; using all 67 beds is the only operationally coherent response to the 70-patient surge.
raw_step.state_change: Final recommendation was fixed as the maximum feasible in-scope allocation with explicit overflow handling.

task: Advanced task: Allocate an incoming surge of 70 patients over 72 hours among three hospitals (A, B, C) using only the supplied data and rules. Data: A beds=220 occupied=198 staff_ratio=5.2 patients/nurse; B beds=160 occupied=121 ratio=4.8; C beds=90 occupied=84 ratio=6.1. Rules: occupancy > 90% is a high-risk occupancy flag; staff_ratio > 5.5 is a staffing risk flag. Distribute the surge to minimize the number of hospitals flagged for high-risk occupancy or staffing, subject to physical bed capacity and the supplied values. Produce: (1) an allocation plan (spreadsheet-ready) showing how many additional patients each hospital should accept and resulting occupancy and staff ratios; (2) a technical memo explaining intermediate calculations, validation checks, uncertainty/sensitivity notes, caveats, and a final decision table tying every recommendation back to the supplied values. All calculations must be reproducible from the embedded packet alone (no external data lookups).
